verses.text: If one be found slain in the field, and it be not known who slew him — the elders and judges shall measure unto the cities round about; the city next unto the slain shall take an heifer never yoked, bring her to a rough valley with running water, strike off her neck. The elders shall wash their hands over her, say: Our hands have not shed this blood, our eyes have not seen. Be merciful, O LORD; lay not innocent blood to thy people's charge. The blood shall be forgiven them. Guidance: A body in the field with no killer. The community bears the question even when no one bears the guilt. The unsolved death matters to God; the closest town carries the ritual. Washed hands, broken neck, and a corporate prayer that no innocent blood stay on the nation's ledger.

When thou goest forth to war, hast taken captives, seest a beautiful woman, hast a desire to have her to wife — bring her home; she shall shave her head, pare her nails, put away the raiment of her captivity; bewail her father and mother a full month; after that thou shalt be her husband. If thou have no delight in her, thou shalt let her go whither she will; not sell her for money, because thou hast humbled her. Guidance: A captive bride was protected by a deliberate one-month grief period — she becomes a person before she becomes a wife. The man cannot sell her later if it doesn't work out. The text doesn't romanticize the situation; it dignifies the woman caught inside it. Even ancient wartime gets a humanizing line.

If a man have two wives, one beloved, another hated, and the firstborn son be hers that was hated: when he maketh his sons to inherit, he may not make the son of the beloved firstborn before the son of the hated. He shall acknowledge the son of the hated for the firstborn, by giving him a double portion: for he is the beginning of his strength; the right of the firstborn is his. Guidance: Birth order trumps preference. The father's affection cannot rewrite the firstborn's deed. God protects the child of the less-loved wife — not on the strength of his mother's charm but on the strength of his birth. Family politics had a ceiling in this law.

If a man have a stubborn and rebellious son, which will not obey his father or his mother — they have chastened him, will not hearken — then his father and mother shall bring him out unto the elders of his city; say: This our son is stubborn and rebellious, a glutton and a drunkard. All the men of his city shall stone him. So shalt thou put evil away. Guidance: A horrifying law. But notice: BOTH parents must agree, the elders investigate, the city judges. Private rage can never trigger it. The text describes a hardened adult son — glutton, drunkard, intractable — not a moody teenager. Rabbinic tradition records that this penalty was never actually carried out. The law existed as the outer limit; mercy worked within it.

If a man have committed a sin worthy of death, hanged on a tree: his body shall not remain all night upon the tree, thou shalt in any wise bury him that day; for he that is hanged is accursed of God; that thy land be not defiled. Guidance: A body on a tree was a curse-sign — bury it before sunset. Paul will read this verse straight through to Calvary: Christ became a curse for us, hanged on a tree to absorb the curse that was on us. Even this brief sundown law has a thousand-year echo.
